title: JWT verification accepts tokens without constraining algorithm or issuer/audience

evidence.quote:
const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
    req.user = decoded;

reasoning: The authentication middleware verifies bearer tokens using only the shared secret and then trusts the decoded payload as req.user. Without explicit allowed algorithms and expected issuer/audience, tokens signed with the same secret for another context, environment, or token class can be accepted by this API if they contain a role field. That weakens authorization boundaries because authorize later trusts req.user.role directly.
recommendation: Pass verification options to jwt.verify, including a fixed algorithm list and expected issuer/audience values, and validate the decoded payload shape before assigning req.user.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No linked auth tests are included, so there is no evidence that cross-context tokens, unexpected algorithms, or malformed payloads are rejected.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add tests that a token signed with the correct secret but wrong audience/issuer is rejected, and that only the configured algorithm is accepted.
minimumFixScope: Update authenticate in src/middleware/auth.js to supply jwt.verify options and validate decoded user claims before authorization uses them.

history.reasoning: The original evidence path still exists at src/middleware/auth.js. The exact original call changed from jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET) to jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] }), so the algorithm portion is partially addressed there. However, issuer and audience are still not constrained, the decoded payload is still assigned directly to req.user, and tokens produced in src/routes/auth.js are still signed without issuer/audience claims. The same vulnerable middleware still exists unchanged at server/src/middleware/auth.js with jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET), and src/index.js also verifies Socket.IO tokens without algorithms/issuer/audience. No test script is defined in package.json, and I did not find auth regression tests proving wrong issuer/audience or malformed payload rejection. Therefore the finding remains open, although the original src/middleware/auth.js evidence has partially changed.
